original_abstract: Globalization has profoundly reshaped societal habits and deep-seated cultural foundations, influencing everything from daily lifestyles to fundamental social norms and aesthetic preferences. Driven by the proliferation of affordable and accessible digital media, individuals can now instantaneously engage with information from across the globe. However, this shift often leads to the uncritical adoption of foreign influences without a thorough consideration of their long-term cultural consequences. Utilizing a qualitative descriptive approach supported by extensive library research, this study explores the multifaceted nature of globalization. The findings suggest that while globalization is an ubiquitous force with both beneficial and detrimental effects, it presents a unique challenge in the digital era: the need for a strategic balance. Ultimately, preserving local cultural diversity requires a nuanced approach that remains open to global integration while proactively safeguarding unique cultural identities against homogenization.
humanized_abstract: According to Globalisation and Local Cultures: Fashioning Asia in Australia as the impact of globalisation on people’s habits and beliefs has become increasingly ubiquitous, an emerging culture which may erode the existing local culture has become more visible. The rapid availability of inexpensive global media or electronic media has made it possible for people all over the world to communicate in real time with others remotely. These global influences are often taken for granted without proper consideration of their ongoing cultural effect. This paper aims to investigate the impact of globalisation from a qualitative descriptive approach with considerable library-based research. In the context of this study, globalisation has been shown to be a worldwide phenomenon and that it carries many advantages and disadvantages. It has made our lives richer but at the same time increased its complexity thereby necessitating a strategic balance in this era of globalisation and new media such as cyber media. So that the local culture can be preserved, people must be aware of the impending cultural challenges brought about by globalisation and must take counteractive measures in order to prevent the loss of local cultural uniqueness while, at the same time, allowing globalisation to carry on.
